_meta.source: codecheck.com (updated Feb 5, 2026), ICC adoption maps, state licensing board websites
_meta.disclaimer: Codes are adopted at state level but amended locally. Always verify current local amendments with the AHJ.

AL.name: Alabama
AL.permit_authority: Local AHJ (city/county). Residential under Home Builders Licensure Board.
AL.notes: No statewide building code for residential. Each county and city adopts independently.
key_quirks: No statewide residential code — wide variation by county
Rural counties may have minimal enforcement

AK.name: Alaska
AK.permit_authority: Anchorage follows state code. Rest of state is per local rules — many rural areas have no code enforcement.
AK.notes: Deep frost lines require special footing depths (60+ inches in some areas). Seismic zone for many areas.
key_quirks: Remote areas often have no permit requirement
Extreme climate — specific cold-weather requirements for HVAC

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.permit_authority: Entirely up to local jurisdictions — varies widely
key_quirks: ROC (Registrar of Contractors) license required for all contractor work
Maricopa County and Tucson have own permit offices with online portals
Phoenix: Development Services Department — strong online permitting
Rural AZ: some areas have very limited code enforcement
Solar: strong market, most cities have streamlined solar permits

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.permit_authority: State code applies to state buildings only. Local jurisdictions control residential.
key_quirks: Many rural areas have minimal permit requirements
Little Rock and Fayetteville have own permit offices

CA.name: California
CA.permit_authority: Statewide minimum — local jurisdictions can be MORE restrictive
key_quirks: LA and San Francisco have amended state code — stricter in many areas
Title 24 energy code applies to all work — HVAC replacements must meet efficiency minimums
Seismic strapping mandatory for water heaters statewide
HVAC: C-20 license | Electrical: C-10 | Plumbing: C-36 | Roofing: C-39 | Solar: C-46
Wildfire (WUI) zones require Class A fire-rated roofing materials
Cool roof requirements in Title 24 climate zones — check before ordering roofing
Many cities use SolarAPP+ for instant solar permits
SoCal air districts may require CARB-compliant generator certification
Deck additions require seismic-appropriate hardware (stainless in coastal zones)

notable_cities.Los Angeles: LADBS — permit times are slow (1–4 weeks for most work). Online portal: ladbs.org
notable_cities.San Francisco: DBI — own code amendments, very strict. portal: sfdbi.org
notable_cities.San Diego: Development Services — roof replacement may NOT require permit under CRC (verify SDMC §129.0202)
notable_cities.Sacramento: CSS — moderate speed, good online portal
notable_cities.Fresno: Development & Resource Management — faster than coastal cities

CO.name: Colorado
CO.permit_authority: Denver and state buildings only. Other jurisdictions have 'home rule' — each city/county sets own codes.
key_quirks: Denver has its own permit system — Denver Community Planning and Development
Colorado Springs, Fort Collins, Boulder all have own permit offices
Mountain towns (Aspen, Vail) — very strict, slow permit process, high fees
Front Range: Class 4 hail-resistant roofing common due to insurance requirements
Snow load requirements important for roofing and deck projects

CT.name: Connecticut
key_quirks: High-cost Northeast market — permit fees and contractor rates are elevated
Strong enforcement in most municipalities

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.permit_authority: DCRA (Department of Consumer and Regulatory Affairs)
key_quirks: Older code editions still in effect — 2014 NEC, 2015 IRC
Slow permit process — DC is notorious for delays
DCRA: dcra.dc.gov

DE.name: Delaware
key_quirks: Dover, Wilmington have own permit offices
Many Delaware counties have limited enforcement in rural areas

FL.name: Florida
FL.permit_authority: Statewide Florida Building Code — very strictly enforced
key_quirks: Florida Building Code is among the strictest in the US — post-Hurricane Andrew legacy
Hurricane-rated products required in HVHZ (High Velocity Hurricane Zone): Broward and Miami-Dade counties
Wind mitigation reports affect insurance — often requested at permit
Roofing: specific nail patterns (6d vs 8d), specific underlayment requirements by wind zone
HVAC: CAC license required statewide. Strong enforcement.
All permits must be recorded and easily accessible to inspectors
Permit cards must be posted at job site
Solar: PE stamp often required. Strong market.
Generator: standby generators common — setback requirements strict

notable_cities.Miami-Dade: Strictest jurisdiction in FL — HVHZ requirements, Miami-Dade Product Approval required for many materials
notable_cities.Orlando: Orange County Building Division — good online portal, moderate speed
notable_cities.Tampa: Hillsborough County — online permits available, moderate speed
notable_cities.Jacksonville: City of Jacksonville Building Inspection Division

GA.name: Georgia
GA.permit_authority: Statewide minimum codes. Georgia Department of Community Affairs.
key_quirks: Atlanta metro has fast online permitting in many cities (Marietta, Sandy Springs, Alpharetta)
NEC 2023 and IRC 2024 — some of the most current codes in the US
Rural GA: limited enforcement in some counties

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.permit_authority: County-level (Honolulu, Maui, Hawaii County, Kauai) — each has own DPP
key_quirks: Seismic and hurricane zone — strict structural requirements
Solar is extremely popular — permitting infrastructure is well-developed
Honolulu: DPP — permit times can be slow (2–4 weeks)

ID.name: Idaho
ID.permit_authority: Statewide building code — Division of Building Safety
key_quirks: Boise metro growing fast — permit offices can be slow due to growth
Extensive NEC amendments — verify locally

IL.name: Illinois
IL.permit_authority: Local jurisdictions adopt 'current' editions — varies widely. No statewide code.
key_quirks: CHICAGO: Has own building code — Chicago Building Code. Strict. 2017 NEC, 2018 IRC with Chicago amendments.
Chicago: Licensed Electrician required for all electrical. City-specific licenses sometimes required.
Suburban Cook County: generally follows state recommendations
Downstate IL: varies widely — some rural counties have minimal enforcement
Chicago permit portal: chicago.gov/permits

IN.name: Indiana
IN.nec_edition: 2008 (very old — statewide for some work, local for others)
IN.permit_authority: Statewide for some uses, requires local adoption. Uses IRC electrical (not NEC separately).
key_quirks: 2008 NEC is surprisingly old — most cities have adopted newer editions locally
Indianapolis (Marion County) has own permit office — moderate enforcement
Indiana Professional Licensing Agency: pla.in.gov
Many smaller cities have limited building departments

IA.name: Iowa
key_quirks: Current codes — NEC 2023, IRC 2024. Well-maintained code adoption.
Des Moines has modern online permitting

KS.name: Kansas
KS.permit_authority: No statewide code — local jurisdictions. 2008 NEC for state-owned buildings only.
key_quirks: Wichita-Sedgwick County has own permit office
Wide variation across rural Kansas — some counties have no enforcement

KY.name: Kentucky
key_quirks: Uses own Kentucky Plumbing Code — NOT IRC plumbing
No IRC electrical — uses own electrical code
Louisville Metro has own permit office
Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction: dhbc.ky.gov

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.permit_authority: Statewide. Louisiana State Uniform Construction Code.
key_quirks: Hurricane zone — wind requirements similar to Florida Gulf Coast
New Orleans has own permit office — HDLC in historic districts adds another layer
Flood zone requirements important for many parishes

ME.name: Maine
ME.permit_authority: Enforceable only for cities with population over 4,000. Many rural areas have no code enforcement.
key_quirks: Many rural areas have no permit requirement
Portland and Bangor have active building departments
Deep frost lines (42-48 inches typical)

MD.name: Maryland
MD.permit_authority: Statewide minimum, but counties set own NEC edition and amendments.
key_quirks: Montgomery County and Prince George's County (DC suburbs) have own permit offices — strict
Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) license required
Baltimore City has own building code and permit office

MA.name: Massachusetts
key_quirks: High-cost Northeast market
Board of Building Regulations and Standards (BBRS)
Licensed Electrician required for ALL electrical work — strictly enforced
Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration required for most work
Boston has own Inspectional Services Department — can be slow

MI.name: Michigan
key_quirks: Residential on 2015 IRC, commercial on 2021 IBC — gap between editions
Michigan Residential Code (MRC) is primary residential reference
Detroit has own permit office — City of Detroit Buildings, Safety Engineering and Environmental Department (BSEED)
Grand Rapids, Lansing have own offices

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.permit_authority: Statewide. Minnesota Department of Labor and Industry.
key_quirks: 2026 NEC currently under review — update coming
Extensive state amendments to NEC — the Minnesota State Electrical Code (MSEC) differs from NEC in meaningful ways
Licensed Electrical Contractor required
Minneapolis and St. Paul have own permit offices but follow state code
Deep frost lines (42-54 inches) — important for footings and underground work

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.permit_authority: Local level. Mississippi Insurance Department enforces some requirements.
key_quirks: Wide variation — some counties have no permit enforcement
Hurricane zone along Gulf Coast — strict wind requirements in coastal counties

MO.name: Missouri
MO.permit_authority: Local level. St. Louis example given as reference — varies by county.
key_quirks: Kansas City and St. Louis have own permit offices and stricter enforcement
Most of Missouri is local jurisdiction — rural areas may have minimal enforcement
Missouri HVAC Authority: missourihvacauthority.com for HVAC license info

MT.name: Montana
MT.permit_authority: Local jurisdictions have option to enforce. Many rural areas do not.
key_quirks: Billings, Missoula, Bozeman have active building departments
Many rural areas have no permit requirement
Deep frost lines in northern MT

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.permit_authority: Electrical is statewide. Other codes per local jurisdictions.
key_quirks: Electrical code is statewide — NEC 2023 applies everywhere
Omaha and Lincoln have own building permit offices for non-electrical work

NV.name: Nevada
key_quirks: Clark County (Las Vegas area) has own building department — Southern Nevada Building Officials
Washoe County (Reno area) — separate permit office
Solar: very active market — streamlined permits in Clark County
NEC 2023 statewide — current

NH.name: New Hampshire
key_quirks: NH Office of Professional Licensure and Certification (OPLC)
Frost line: 42-48 inches in most of state

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.permit_authority: Statewide — NJ Uniform Construction Code (UCC). DCA.
key_quirks: Uses National Standard Plumbing Code (NSPC) — not IPC
Strong enforcement statewide — NJ is known for strict code compliance
2024 legislation: standby generators can be installed without zoning board approval in residential zones
Solar: active market with strong NJBPU incentives
Licensed trades required: Electrical (NJ Electrical Contractor), Plumbing (NJ Master Plumber)

NM.name: New Mexico
key_quirks: Albuquerque and Santa Fe have own permit offices but follow state code
Construction Industries Division: rld.state.nm.us

NY.name: New York
NY.permit_authority: Statewide except NYC (which has its own code). NYC is extremely distinct.
key_quirks: NYC: Licensed Master Electrician required for ALL electrical permit work. Very strict.
NYC: Licensed Master Plumber required for gas and plumbing work.
NYC permit process can take 2–6 weeks for most work — plan ahead
NYC DOB: nyc.gov/buildings
Upstate NY: follows statewide code, much faster permit process
Long Island: Nassau and Suffolk counties — active enforcement, reasonable permit speed

NC.name: North Carolina
key_quirks: Older IRC edition (2015) — code updates delayed post-hurricane response
Charlotte and Raleigh have active, efficient permit offices
Coastal counties: wind zone requirements important
NC Licensing Board for General Contractors: nclbgc.org

ND.name: North Dakota
key_quirks: Current code editions
Very deep frost lines in some areas (42-54 inches)

OH.name: Ohio
OH.permit_authority: Statewide. Ohio Board of Building Standards.
key_quirks: 2021 IBC for commercial; 2018 IRC for residential
Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati all have own permit offices
Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board (OCILB) for mechanical/electrical licenses

OK.name: Oklahoma
key_quirks: Oklahoma City and Tulsa have own permit offices
Tornado zone — specific anchoring requirements

OR.name: Oregon
key_quirks: 2024 codes go into effect April 1, 2026
Portland: Bureau of Development Services — known for slow permit times
Seismic zone (Cascadia Subduction Zone) — seismic requirements for all work
Oregon CCB (Construction Contractors Board) license required

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.permit_authority: Most jurisdictions opt in to UCC (Uniform Construction Code). Philadelphia has custom code.
key_quirks: Philadelphia: own City of Philadelphia code, own L&I (Licenses and Inspections) department
Philadelphia permits are notoriously slow — plan 3–8 weeks for anything complex
Pittsburgh: Allegheny County enforcement, moderate speed
PA Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration required for work over $5,000

RI.name: Rhode Island
key_quirks: Small state — most work processed through local building departments
Providence has own permit office

SC.name: South Carolina
key_quirks: SC LLR (Department of Labor, Licensing and Regulation)
Coastal counties: wind zone requirements for roofing and HVAC

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.permit_authority: Local jurisdictions have option to adopt
key_quirks: Many rural areas have no permit requirement
Sioux Falls and Rapid City have active building departments

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.irc_edition: 2021 (residential may still be on 2018 IRC in some areas)
key_quirks: Nashville: own Metro Codes Department — active enforcement, good online portal
Memphis: own codes office
NEC 2017 — older electrical code
TN Department of Commerce & Insurance — licensing

TX.name: Texas
TX.nec_edition: 2020 (NFPA says 2023 NEC for some jurisdictions — varies widely)
TX.permit_authority: Local jurisdictions control everything. No statewide residential building code outside city limits.
key_quirks: Major cities (Houston, Dallas, Austin, San Antonio) have strong permit offices
Unincorporated areas (no city limits): often NO permit required
HVAC: TACL license (Texas Air Conditioning Contractor License) or HVAC-R license from TDLR
Electrical: TECL license from TDLR. Master Electrician required for permit pulls.
Plumbing: Master Plumber license from TSBPE
Houston: No zoning, but strong building permit enforcement. Houston Permitting Center.
Austin: Strong sustainability requirements — Austin Energy Green Building
Solar: popular — no statewide solar license, use licensed electrician for electrical portion
Generator: high demand post-winter storm 2021 — local enforcement varies widely

notable_cities.Houston: Houston Permitting Center: houstonpermittingcenter.org — online permits available

UT.name: Utah
UT.permit_authority: State buildings — local adoptions may lag. Salt Lake City and major cities follow current codes.
key_quirks: Salt Lake City: growing fast, modern permit office
Seismic zone (Wasatch Fault) — seismic requirements apply
Utah Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing (DOPL)

VT.name: Vermont
VT.permit_authority: Statewide. Adopts IBC (not IRC) for all construction.
key_quirks: Uses IBC for ALL construction — no IRC for residential
Vermont Department of Public Safety — Fire and Building Safety Division
Deep frost lines (42 inches)

VA.name: Virginia
key_quirks: Northern Virginia (NOVA): Fairfax, Arlington, Alexandria — strict, active enforcement
DPOR (Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation) for licensing
Richmond and Virginia Beach have own permit offices

WA.name: Washington
WA.permit_authority: Statewide. Washington State Building Code Council.
key_quirks: Update scheduled for May 2026 — new codes coming
Seismic zone (Pacific Northwest) — seismic requirements for all work
Seattle: Seattle DCI (Department of Construction and Inspections) — known for thorough inspections
Seattle permit times: moderate to slow (2–4 weeks for complex work)
WA Electrical License required (EL01A/EL02A). Plumbing license from LNI.
Strong energy code (WSEC) — HVAC replacements must meet efficiency minimums
EV charger infrastructure: WA leading state for EV adoption

WV.name: West Virginia
key_quirks: WV Division of Labor
Charleston and Huntington have active building departments

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.permit_authority: Statewide — Wisconsin Uniform Dwelling Code (UDC). DHS.
key_quirks: Wisconsin has own modified code — NOT the IRC or IBC directly
Older NEC (2017) and IRC (2015) base — Wisconsin code updates slowly
Wisconsin SPS (Safety and Professional Services) for licensing
Milwaukee has own permit office
Deep frost lines (42-54 inches)

WY.name: Wyoming
key_quirks: Many rural areas have no permit requirement
Cheyenne and Casper have active building departments
High altitude considerations for HVAC sizing
